danger_signals.visual_form: Animated hostile entities in level spaces
danger_signals.meaning: Areas marked as unsafe; direct contact causes health loss

danger_signals.visual_form: Vertical drops and chasms in terrain
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger; falling from great heights deals damage
Progression blocked until specific Viking skill or item used

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy arrows and projectiles in flight
danger_signals.meaning: Ranged damage threat; Olaf's shield required for blocking

safety_signals.meaning: Restores health points when picked up

safety_signals.visual_form: Designated end-zone location (implied from gameplay description)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe completion state when all three Vikings reach it
Temporary safety from enemies when using Olaf's shield mechanics

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Alien enemies representing Tomator's forces across time periods
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Futuristic/technological aesthetic in early stages; shifts by level theme

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Three distinct character silhouettes: Erik (Swift), Baleog (Fierce), Olaf (Stout)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scandinavian/Norse thematic elements; escalate through time-displaced worlds

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shifting environmental themes: tech-alien, time-period variations, increasingly bizarre locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy types and obstacle complexity scale; Viking coordination requirements increase

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Health indicator (3 points per Viking; visual loss when hit)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Damaged Viking shows reduced capability; death removes character from active play, making level potentially unwinnable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Collectible objects (keys, bombs, food) visible in world and carried by Vikings
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Items swappable between close characters; certain items required to progress past locked areas

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Diverse environmental settings across anachronistic periods
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Vikings must escape increasingly complex puzzle-arena spaces designed by Tomator

environmental_storytelling.example: Three Vikings displayed as distinct sprites with unique identities (Erik the Swift emphasizes speed/agility, Baleog the Fierce carries weapons, Olaf the Stout bears a shield)
Levels traverse 'various periods of time' with visual environment shifts signaling narrative progression through Croutonian kidnapping escape
Walls require Erik's helmet bash, switches need Baleog's bow, heights need Olaf's shield as platform—visual design telegraphs which Viking is needed
Game design requires all three Vikings reach exit; spatial level layouts show how each character's unique ability enables others' progression

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Runs faster than peers; can bash through destructible walls and weaker enemies with helmet
Attacks enemies in melee with sword; engages ranged targets with bow and unlimited arrows; can trigger distant switches
Blocks enemy attacks and projectiles with shield; converts shield into hang-glider for descent/traversal; shield serves as platform for allies

entity_behavioral_tells.visual_manifestation: Sprite shows damage indication; health counter decrements
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Movement/action capability may reduce; one more hit triggers death and removal from level
Removed from active play; level becomes unwinnable if all three die or if essential skill-user falls; player must restart or continue
Spawn in level spaces with attack patterns; projectile-firing enemies require blocking (Olaf) or evasion; melee enemies fall to Baleog's sword or Erik's bash

notes: The Lost Vikings uses high-contrast, vibrant color palette (console television optimization). Visual communication relies on sprite clarity and distinct character silhouettes to convey three-Viking puzzle-platformer identity. World-state shifts through environmental theming (tech/alien → time-period variations → endgame complexity) rather than dynamic visual effects. Danger/safety signals implicit in obstacle placement and enemy type variation per level. No documented faction color schemes; factions distinguished by narrative role (kidnapper vs. escapee) rather than visual insignia. Cooperative mechanics emphasized through shared-exit requirement and spatial skill-gating.
sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (Lost Vikings SNES)
wikipedia - The Lost Vikings (gameplay, character abilities, plot, development)
